solution: By the chain rule, \( \dfrac{dz}{dt} = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}\dfrac{dx}{dt} + \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y}\dfrac{dy}{dt} = 2[[A]]xy\cdot[[a]] + [[A]]x^{2}\cdot[[c]] \). Substituting \( x([[t0]])=[[a]]\cdot[[t0]]+[[b]] \) and \( y([[t0]])=[[c]]\cdot[[t0]]+[[d]] \) gives \( \dfrac{dz}{dt}\Big|_{t=[[t0]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This keeps only the \( \partial f/\partial y \) branch of the chain rule, dropping the \( \partial f/\partial x \) contribution.
This keeps only the \( \partial f/\partial x \) branch of the chain rule, dropping the \( \partial f/\partial y \) contribution.
This is \( z([[t0]]) \), the value of \( z \) itself, not its rate of change.
This differentiates as if \( x \) and \( y \) were replaced by their slopes everywhere, dropping the actual point values.
This is off by three from the correct value.
This is off by three from the correct value.

stem: Let \( z = f(x,y) = [[A]]x^{2} + [[B]]y \), with \( x(t) = [[R]]\cos(t) \) and \( y(t) = [[R]]\sin(t) \). What is \( \dfrac{dz}{dt} \) at \( t=0 \)?
solution: By the chain rule, \( \dfrac{dz}{dt} = 2[[A]]x\cdot(-[[R]]\sin t) + [[B]]\cdot([[R]]\cos t) \). At \( t=0 \), \( x(0)=[[R]] \), \( \sin(0)=0 \), and \( \cos(0)=1 \), so the first term vanishes and \( \dfrac{dz}{dt}\Big|_{t=0} = [[B]]\cdot[[R]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies all three coefficients together instead of correctly cancelling the vanishing \( \sin(0) \) term.
This has the wrong sign on the surviving term.
This mistakenly evaluates \( 2A\cdot x'(0)\cdot x(0) \) as if the vanishing \( \sin \) factor were a \( \cos \) factor instead.
This reports \( z(0) \) itself (with the wrong coefficient), not the rate of change.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

solution: By the chain rule, \( \dfrac{dz}{dt} = ([[A]]y+[[B]])\dfrac{dx}{dt} + [[A]]x\dfrac{dy}{dt} = ([[A]]y+[[B]])(2[[p]]t) + [[A]]x(3[[q]]t^{2}) \). Substituting \( x([[t0]])=[[p]]\cdot[[t0]]^{2} \), \( y([[t0]])=[[q]]\cdot[[t0]]^{3} \) gives \( \dfrac{dz}{dt}\Big|_{t=[[t0]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This applies the product rule to \( x(t)y(t) \) directly by name but drops the \( [[B]]x \) term's own derivative.
This is \( z([[t0]]) \), the value of \( z \) itself, not its rate of change.
This differentiates \( xy \) as if it were the single power \( t^{5} \) without the product-rule split into two terms.
This doubles both derivative contributions instead of using the correct exponents 2 and 3.
This is off by four from the correct value.
This is off by four from the correct value.

stem: Let \( z = f(x,y) = [[A]]x^{2} + [[B]]xy + [[C]]y^{2} \), where \( x(s,t) = [[p]]s + [[q]]t \) and \( y(s,t) = [[r]]s + [[w]]t \). What is \( \dfrac{\partial z}{\partial s} \) at \( (s,t) = ([[s0]],[[t0]]) \)?
solution: By the (multivariable) chain rule, \( \dfrac{\partial z}{\partial s} = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}\dfrac{\partial x}{\partial s} + \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y}\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial s} = (2[[A]]x+[[B]]y)\cdot[[p]] + ([[B]]x+2[[C]]y)\cdot[[r]] \), where \( x=[[p]]s+[[q]]t \), \( y=[[r]]s+[[w]]t \). Substituting \( (s,t)=([[s0]],[[t0]]) \) gives \( \dfrac{\partial z}{\partial s} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( \partial x/\partial t=[[q]] \) and \( \partial y/\partial t=[[w]] \) instead of \( \partial x/\partial s \) and \( \partial y/\partial s \), computing \( \partial z/\partial t \) by mistake.
This is \( z([[s0]],[[t0]]) \), the value of \( z \) itself, not a partial derivative.
This keeps only the \( f_x \) branch of the chain rule, dropping the \( f_y \) contribution.
This keeps only the \( f_y \) branch of the chain rule, dropping the \( f_x \) contribution.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( z = f(x,y) = \dfrac{x}{y} \), with \( x(t) = [[a]]t + [[b]] \) and \( y(t) = [[c]]t + [[d]] \). What is \( \dfrac{dz}{dt} \) at \( t=[[t0]] \)?
solution: By the chain rule (equivalently the quotient rule), \( \dfrac{dz}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{y}\dfrac{dx}{dt} - \dfrac{x}{y^{2}}\dfrac{dy}{dt} = \dfrac{[[a]]([[c]]t+[[d]]) - [[c]]([[a]]t+[[b]])}{([[c]]t+[[d]])^{2}} \). Evaluating at \( t=[[t0]] \) gives \( \dfrac{dz}{dt}\Big|_{t=[[t0]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This just divides the leading coefficients, ignoring the quotient rule entirely.
This adds instead of subtracts in the quotient-rule numerator.
This is \( z([[t0]]) \), the value of \( z \) itself, not its rate of change.
This uses \( ac \) in the numerator instead of the correct cross-difference \( a([[c]]t+[[d]])-c([[a]]t+[[b]]) \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
